sectionName: Biological & Biochemical Foundations of Living Systems
topicArea: Cell Signaling: Kinases, GPCRs & Signal Amplification

questions.passage:
A cell biology lab studies a hormone that binds a membrane receptor on liver cells. Within seconds, intracellular cAMP rises, protein kinase A becomes active, and glycogen phosphorylase activity increases. A nonhydrolyzable GTP analog prolongs the response, whereas a phosphodiesterase inhibitor increases response duration by slowing cAMP breakdown.

Treatment | cAMP | PKA activity | Glycogen phosphorylase
Hormone | high | high | high
Receptor antagonist | low | low | low
Phosphodiesterase inhibitor | prolonged | prolonged | prolonged

The investigators propose that the receptor is a Gs-coupled GPCR activating adenylyl cyclase.
questions.stem: Which enzyme most directly produces cAMP in the hormone pathway?

explanations.A: Correct. Adenylyl cyclase converts ATP to cAMP after activation by Gsα. PKA responds to cAMP, phosphodiesterase degrades it, and glycogen phosphorylase is a downstream metabolic target.
explanations.B: PKA is downstream of cAMP and cannot explain the initial rise in the second messenger.
explanations.C: Phosphodiesterase lowers cAMP by hydrolysis, so inhibiting it prolongs signaling rather than producing cAMP.
explanations.D: Glycogen phosphorylase acts on glycogen metabolism; it is activated downstream of the signaling cascade.

questions.topic: Cell Signaling: Kinases, GPCRs & Signal Amplification

questions.stem: Why does a nonhydrolyzable GTP analog prolong signaling?

choices.label: B
choices.text: It prevents Gα from turning itself off by GTP hydrolysis

explanations.A: Destroying ligand would shorten receptor activation rather than prolong the intracellular response.
explanations.B: Correct. G proteins are active when bound to GTP and switch off when GTP is hydrolyzed to GDP. A nonhydrolyzable analog traps Gα in an active-like state, extending downstream signaling.
explanations.C: Instant cAMP degradation would lower PKA activity, the opposite of a prolonged response.
explanations.D: The pathway requires kinase activity downstream; blocking all kinases would suppress, not prolong, signaling.

questions.topic: Cell Signaling: Kinases, GPCRs & Signal Amplification

questions.stem: What is the effect of phosphodiesterase inhibition?

explanations.A: Shortening would occur if phosphodiesterase activity increased, not if it were inhibited.
explanations.B: The passage directly shows prolonged cAMP, so saying no effect ignores the treatment table.
explanations.C: Correct. Phosphodiesterase terminates cAMP signaling by converting cAMP to AMP. Inhibiting it slows cAMP breakdown, so PKA activity and downstream effects persist longer.
explanations.D: Receptor dimerization describes receptor tyrosine kinases, not the cAMP degradation step.

questions.topic: Cell Signaling: Kinases, GPCRs & Signal Amplification

questions.passage: A second pathway is triggered by a growth factor receptor with intrinsic tyrosine kinase activity. Ligand binding causes receptor dimerization, autophosphorylation, recruitment of adaptor proteins, and activation of a Ras-MAPK cascade. A kinase-dead receptor binds ligand but fails to autophosphorylate. A constitutively active Ras mutant increases downstream ERK phosphorylation even when the receptor is blocked.
questions.stem: Which feature best distinguishes the growth factor receptor from the GPCR?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It is intracellular and binds steroid

choices.label: C
choices.text: It uses cAMP as its only second messenger

choices.label: D
choices.text: It has intrinsic or associated tyrosine kinase signaling after dimerization

explanations.A: The passage describes a membrane growth factor receptor, not an intracellular steroid receptor.
explanations.B: Glycogen hydrolysis is metabolic output in the GPCR passage, not the receptor's catalytic activity.
explanations.C: The RTK passage emphasizes Ras-MAPK, not cAMP as the only second messenger.
explanations.D: Correct. Receptor tyrosine kinases dimerize and autophosphorylate tyrosine residues, creating docking sites for signaling proteins. That differs from GPCR signaling through heterotrimeric G proteins.

questions.topic: Cell Signaling: Kinases, GPCRs & Signal Amplification

questions.stem: Why does constitutively active Ras bypass receptor blockade?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Ras acts downstream of the receptor and can drive MAPK signaling without upstream activation

explanations.A: Ras is an intracellular small GTPase, not the extracellular growth factor ligand.
explanations.B: Correct. If Ras is locked active downstream of the receptor, blocking receptor activation no longer prevents signaling to ERK. This is classic epistasis logic: downstream activation bypasses upstream inhibition.
explanations.C: Active Ras increases ERK phosphorylation in the passage; it does not degrade ERK phosphorylation.
explanations.D: Ras activity depends on GTP binding. Preventing GTP binding would inactivate Ras rather than make it constitutively active.

questions.topic: Cell Signaling: Kinases, GPCRs & Signal Amplification

questions.stem: What does receptor autophosphorylation accomplish in the RTK pathway?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Creates phosphotyrosine docking sites for adaptor proteins

choices.label: B
choices.text: Turns the receptor into a GPCR

choices.label: C
choices.text: Removes all phosphate groups from ATP

explanations.A: Correct. Autophosphorylated tyrosines serve as recognition sites for SH2/PTB-domain proteins, organizing downstream signaling complexes such as the Ras-MAPK cascade.
explanations.B: A receptor tyrosine kinase does not become a GPCR; the receptor classes remain mechanistically distinct.
explanations.C: Kinase activity transfers phosphate groups from ATP to protein residues; it does not remove all phosphate from ATP nonspecifically.
explanations.D: Ligand binding causes dimerization and activation; autophosphorylation is a consequence that propagates signal.

questions.topic: Cell Signaling: Kinases, GPCRs & Signal Amplification

questions.stem: A kinase-dead receptor binds ligand but fails to signal because it cannot:

choices.label: A
choices.text: enter the nucleus as a transcription factor

choices.label: C
choices.text: autophosphorylate and recruit downstream signaling proteins

explanations.A: RTKs signal from the membrane through cascades; they do not need to enter the nucleus as the receptor itself.
explanations.B: Ligand binding is preserved by definition in the kinase-dead receptor, so this cannot explain failure after ligand exposure.
explanations.C: Correct. The passage states ligand binding remains intact, but autophosphorylation fails. Without phosphotyrosine docking sites, adaptor recruitment and downstream MAPK activation are impaired.
explanations.D: cAMP hydrolysis belongs to phosphodiesterases in GPCR pathways, not kinase-dead RTK signaling.

questions.topic: Cell Signaling: Kinases, GPCRs & Signal Amplification

questions.stem: Which result would place MEK downstream of Ras?

choices.label: A
choices.text: MEK is secreted as the growth factor

choices.label: C
choices.text: MEK inhibition increases receptor autophosphorylation only

choices.label: D
choices.text: Active Ras fails to increase ERK when MEK is inhibited

explanations.A: MEK is an intracellular kinase in the MAPK cascade, not the extracellular ligand.
explanations.B: Ligand binding occurs at the receptor and should not depend on MEK, a downstream kinase.
explanations.C: Changing receptor autophosphorylation alone would suggest upstream feedback, not a required downstream link to ERK.
explanations.D: Correct. If Ras activation cannot raise ERK phosphorylation when MEK is inhibited, MEK is required downstream of Ras to transmit the signal to ERK.

questions.topic: Cell Signaling: Kinases, GPCRs & Signal Amplification

questions.stem: Why can signaling cascades amplify a small extracellular signal?

choices.label: A
choices.text: One activated component can activate many downstream molecules

choices.label: B
choices.text: Each receptor activates exactly one final protein only

choices.label: D
choices.text: Kinases cannot phosphorylate more than one substrate

explanations.A: Correct. Cascades amplify because active enzymes and second messengers can influence multiple targets. One receptor can activate many G proteins, many cAMP molecules, or multiple kinase substrates.
explanations.B: A strictly one-to-one chain would limit amplification and does not fit GPCR or kinase cascade biology.
explanations.C: Second messengers often promote enzyme activation, as cAMP activates PKA in the passage.
explanations.D: Kinases commonly phosphorylate multiple substrate molecules, enabling amplification.

questions.topic: Cell Signaling: Kinases, GPCRs & Signal Amplification

questions.stem: Which process most directly terminates the GPCR signal upstream of cAMP degradation?

explanations.A: ERK nuclear entry belongs to the RTK pathway and is downstream transcriptional signaling, not GPCR shutoff.
explanations.B: Correct. GTP hydrolysis returns Gα to its inactive GDP-bound state, reducing adenylyl cyclase stimulation. This terminates the signal upstream of phosphodiesterase-mediated cAMP breakdown.
explanations.C: Permanently active Ras would sustain MAPK signaling, not terminate the GPCR cAMP pathway.
explanations.D: Glycogen phosphorylase is a metabolic effector; its action does not switch off the receptor-proximal G protein.

questions.topic: Cell Signaling: Kinases, GPCRs & Signal Amplification
